1: Psalm 146 opens the grand Hallel finale of the Psalter (Pss 146–150), five psalms all beginning and ending with hallĕlû-Yāh (Praise the LORD!). This doxological finale represents the Psalter's final movement: from lament (many psalms in Books I–IV) through instruction and wisdom (Ps 119) and the Songs of Ascent (Pss 120–134) to pure praise that increasingly loses its personal and historical specificity and becomes the eschatological voice of all creation. "Praise the LORD, O my soul (hallĕlî napšî et-YHWH)!" — the self-address in praise, urging one's own soul to praise; the same formula opens Ps 103 and 104, suggesting this cluster was shaped as a sequence.
2: "I will praise the LORD all my life; I will sing praises to my God as long as I live" — the two time-spans: "all my life (bĕḥayyāy)" and "as long as I live (bĕʿôdî, while I still exist)." The vow to praise for the entire duration of life is one of the Psalter's characteristic commitments (Pss 63:4; 104:33). In the context of vv. 3–4 (the mortality of princes), the vow to praise "all my life" is framed by the recognition that life is limited — the vow covers exactly as much time as one has, not more. The contrast with "all generations (lĕdôr wādôr, v. 10)" makes the human lifetime modest within the eternal divine reign.
3: "Do not put your trust in princes or in any human being, for there is no salvation in them" — al-tibĕṭĕḥû bĕndîbîm bĕben-ʾādām ʾăšer ʾên lô tĕšûʿāh. The anti-patronage principle. In the ANE patron-client social system, attaching oneself to a powerful nādîb (noble, prince, generous one) was the standard route to protection, resources, and advancement. The entire social order was organized around patron hierarchies. The psalm inverts this: there is "no salvation (tĕšûʿāh)" in any human patron, however powerful. Tĕšûʿāh (salvation, deliverance, victory) is specifically a divine attribute; to look for it in princes is a category error about where salvation comes from. Isaiah 31:1–3 makes the same argument against relying on Egypt.
4: "When his breath departs, he returns to the earth; on that very day his plans come to nothing" — tēṣēʾ rûḥô yāšub lĕʾadmātô bayyôm hahûʾ ʾābĕdû ʿeštonōtāyw. The mortality argument against patronage: the prince's breath (rûaḥ) departs (an echo of Gen 2:7; the same breath that constituted the living creature returns to God, Eccl 12:7; Ps 104:29). "On that very day (bayyôm hahûʾ) his plans come to nothing" — the plans (ʿeštonōt, from šānat, to deliberate, plan) of the most powerful patrons vaporize at the moment of death. The urgency of the warning: those who rely on mortal patrons are relying on someone whose plans will perish on a specific future day.
5: "Blessed is the one whose help is the God of Jacob, whose hope is in the LORD his God" — the beatitude that contrasts with the anti-patronage warning: the one whose patron is YHWH (specifically "the God of Jacob," the covenant God of the specific family who became Israel) is "blessed (ʾašrê)." The beatitude formula invites the hearer to recognize what constitutes genuine flourishing: not the client of the powerful prince but the client of YHWH. "God of Jacob (ʾĕlōhê yaʿăqōb)" — the patriarchal title emphasizing covenant continuity: the same God who chose Jacob and wrestled with him at Peniel (Gen 32:24–30) is the patron of the present worshipper.
6: "who made heaven and earth, the sea and everything in them, and who keeps faith forever" — ʿōśēh šāmayim wāʾāreṣ ʾet-hayyām wĕʾet-kol-ʾăšer-bām. The Creator's credentials as a reliable patron: YHWH made heaven and earth, sea and all that fills them — the comprehensive four-part inventory of creation (Exod 20:11; Neh 9:6; Ps 115:15; 124:8). The one who created everything is the most powerful patron imaginable — and "keeps faith forever (šōmēr ʾĕmet lĕʿôlām)": covenant faithfulness (ʾĕmet) maintained not for a human lifetime but eternally. The contrast with the prince whose plans perish on the day of his death is the point.
7: "who secures justice for the oppressed and gives food to the hungry. The LORD sets prisoners free" — the divine action catalogue of vv. 7–9 is one of the most concentrated lists of YHWH's covenant actions in the OT. It covers: justice for the oppressed (ʿōśēh mišpāṭ laʿăšûqîm), food for the hungry (nōtēn leḥem lāreʿēbîm), freedom for prisoners (YHWH mattîr ʾăsûrîm). Each divine act inverts a human situation of powerlessness. The sequence is closely paralleled in Isaiah 61:1–3 (the servant's mission: "to proclaim freedom for the captives... to release the oppressed") and in Jesus's answer to John the Baptist (Matt 11:4–5; Luke 7:22: "the blind receive sight, the lame walk, lepers are cleansed, the deaf hear, the dead are raised, and the good news is proclaimed to the poor").
8: "the LORD gives sight to the blind, the LORD lifts up those who are bowed down, the LORD loves the righteous" — the divine action catalogue continues: sight for the blind (YHWH pōqēaḥ ʿiwwĕrîm), lifting the bowed (YHWH zôqēp kĕpûpîm), love for the righteous (YHWH ʾōhēb ṣaddîqîm). "Sight for the blind" was not ordinarily achieved through medical means in the ancient world — it was a divine act (Exod 4:11; John 9:39–41). Jesus's healing of the blind is therefore a direct enactment of YHWH's Ps 146:8 claim — the sign of the messianic era's arrival (Matt 9:27–31; 11:5; John 9). "Bowed down (kĕpûpîm)" — the same word as Ps 145:14, describing those bent under the weight of affliction.
9: "The LORD watches over the alien resident; he supports the fatherless and the widow; but the way of the wicked he overturns" — the three-fold vulnerable group formula: the gēr (alien resident, foreigner living within Israel without full citizenship rights), the yātôm (fatherless, orphan), and the ʾalmānāh (widow). This triad is one of the most repeated social-justice formulae of the Torah and prophets (Exod 22:21–22; Deut 10:18; 14:29; 24:17–21; 27:19; Jer 7:6; 22:3; Zech 7:10; Mal 3:5). YHWH's particular care for these three unprotected groups is the covenant's most concrete social obligation, and its violation is singled out for the most severe prophetic condemnation.
10: "The LORD will reign forever — your God, O Zion, for all generations. Praise the LORD!" — yimlōk YHWH lĕʿôlām ʾĕlōhayik ṣiyyôn lĕdōr wādōr hallĕlû-Yāh. The only occurrence in the Psalter of this precise eschatological form: yimlōk YHWH (YHWH will reign/shall reign, future tense), as distinguished from the enthronement psalms' YHWH malak (YHWH has become king, or YHWH reigns, perfect/present). The future-looking assertion closes the psalm and opens the door to the remaining Hallel psalms: the eternal reign of YHWH is both present reality and forward promise. "Your God, O Zion (ʾĕlōhayik ṣiyyôn)" — Zion as the community of the covenant people, not merely the geographic city. The final "Praise the LORD (hallĕlû-Yāh)!" begins the chain of praise that continues through Psalm 150.
